Location: Nanda Devi National Park, Uttarakhand, India
Impact: Speculated glacial lake outburst flood was actually triggered by a landslide, not glacier break-off
Cause of Failure: Large rock and ice avalanche from Ronti peak caused flooding in Chamoli district, affecting Rishiganga, Dhauliganga, and Alaknanda rivers
Current Status: Ongoing recovery and investigation efforts
Casualties: Over 200 killed or missing, most were workers at Tapovan dam site

Location: Ajkai Timföldgyár alumina plant, Ajka, Veszprém County, Hungary
Impact: 40 square kilometers of land affected, spill reached the Danube
Construction Details: Dam of reservoir number 10 collapsed, releasing one million cubic meters of liquid waste from red mud lakes
Cause of Failure: Collapse of dam, release of red mud wave, flooding nearby localities
Historical Significance: Industrial accident, human error suspected, waste product of Bayer process
Current Status: Dam failure, investigations ongoing

Impact: Loss of life, property damage, lack of state regulations for dam construction
Construction Details: Concrete gravity dam built in 1909, 50 feet high, 540 feet long, cost $86,000
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure on September 30, 1911, due to design flaws and inadequate construction
Historical Significance: Largest dam of its type in Pennsylvania at the time, no state regulations for dam building
Current Status: Dam failed in 1911, no longer exists
Casualties: 78 deaths, significant destruction in Freeman Run Valley

Location: Baldwin Hills, South Los Angeles, California
Impact: Vigorous rescue efforts, reservoir now part of Kenneth Hahn State Recreation Area
Construction Details: Baldwin Hills Reservoir constructed between 1947 and 1951 on an active fault line
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure due to lining failure and leakage, 250 million US gallons released
Historical Significance: Subject of continuing interest in civil engineering, built during post-WWII dam construction boom
Current Status: Former reservoir now part of a state recreation area

Impact: 26 homes destroyed, 8 major damage, 8 minor damage, 25 mobile homes destroyed, 1 minor damage
Construction Details: Earthen dam, 1890 feet long, 51.3 feet high, embankment failed through piping near principal spillway
Cause of Failure: Embankment failure due to piping, peak breach flow of 147,000 ft3/s, released 14,200 ac-ft of water
Historical Significance: Dam failure, no historical significance
Current Status: Dam embankment largely intact, no human casualties, 104 structures damaged or destroyed

Location: Otter Tail County, Minnesota, United States
Impact: Failure caused the collapse of four other downstream dams, significant property damage
Construction Details: Concrete hydroelectric gravity dam on the Otter Tail River, constructed in 1908
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to built on top of a spring without proper site evaluations, undermined foundations
Historical Significance: Only flood recorded in the history of Fergus Falls, ruins lie along the Otter Tail River State Water Trail
Current Status: Ruins of the dam remain at the recreational Otter Tail River State Water Trail, no longer operational

Impact: Destruction of offices, cafeteria, houses, farms, inns, and roads
Construction Details: Dam I at the Córrego do Feijão iron ore mine, owned by Vale, released a mudflow downstream
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure of tailings dam, mudflow through mine offices, houses, farms, and roads
Historical Significance: Second disaster involving Vale, following the 2015 Mariana dam disaster
Current Status: Dam classified as low risk, licensed for waste reuse and closure, ongoing investigations and legal actions

Location: Buffalo Creek, Logan County, West Virginia
Impact: 507 houses, 44 mobile homes, 30 businesses destroyed, impacting 16 coal towns along Buffalo Creek Hollow
Construction Details: Coal slurry impoundment dam managed by Pittston Coal, declared "satisfactory" by a federal mine inspector
Cause of Failure: Dam #3 failure followed by heavy rains, water overwhelmed dams #2 and #1, unleashing 132 million US gallons of black waste water
Historical Significance: Two commissions investigated the disaster
Current Status: Dam failure led to significant loss of life and property damage

Impact: Flooding of Alagoa Grande and Mulungu towns, three people drowned
Water loss, financial cost, consortium ownership, local population maltreatment
Construction Details: Hydroelectric dam, 200 meters high, part of a complex providing 880 MW, second dam below, capacity of 2 cubic kilometers
Cause of Failure: Break in dam wall leading to water running out, tunnel collapse causing major break
Ownership Details: Campos Novos Energia S.A., CPFL Energia, Companhia Brasileira de Aluminio, Companhia Niquel Tocantins, etc.
Funding Sources: Inter-American Development Bank, Brazilian Development Bank
Timeline of Events: Tunnel collapse in October 2005, major break in June 2006

Location: Rapid City, South Dakota, United States
Impact: Over 1,335 homes and 5,000 automobiles destroyed, $160 million in property damage
Construction Details: Canyon Lake Dam clogged with debris and failed, 15 inches of rain in Black Hills
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to clogging with debris, severe flooding in Rapid City
Historical Significance: Deadliest flood in South Dakota history, one of the deadliest in U.S. history
Current Status: Dam failed during the flood, no longer exists

Impact: Flood reached downtown Denver, remnants and damage still visible in the park
Construction Details: Castlewood Dam built in 1890, suffered a complete collapse following heavy rains
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to heavy rains, 15-foot wall of water rushed down Cherry Creek to Denver
Historical Significance: Unique part of Colorado's history, remnants of the dam still visible
Current Status: Dam completely collapsed, remnants visible in the park

Impact: Dam and lake part of Turtle Creek Recreation Area, used for recreation, $500,000 in damages in 2008 floods
Construction Details: Embankment dam on the Maquoketa River, created Lake Delhi, built between 1922 and 1929 for hydroelectric power production
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to overtopping and subsequent erosion after heavy rain
Historical Significance: Used for hydroelectric power production until 1973, partnership for power plant refurbishment in 2008
Current Status: Dam failed in 2010, plans for power plant refurbishment, cited for minor problems in 2009 inspection

Location: Los Frailes mine, near Aznalcóllar, Seville Province, Andalusia, Spain
Impact: Cleanup operation took three years at an estimated cost of €240 million
Construction Details: Holding dam burst at the Los Frailes mine, releasing 4–5 million cubic meters of mine tailings containing heavy metals
Cause of Failure: Dam failure led to release of acidic tailings into River Agrio and River Guadiamar, main water source for Doñana National Park
Historical Significance: Dam failure impacted Doñana National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Current Status: Cleanup operation completed after three years at a cost of €240 million

Location: Edenville Dam, Mid Michigan, United States
Impact: Major flooding in Midland County, including the city of Midland
Construction Details: Earthen embankment dam at the confluence of the Tittabawassee River and the Tobacco River, forming Wixom Lake, built in 1924, 54 feet high, 6,600 feet long at crest, equipped with two 2.4 MW turbines
Cause of Failure: Breached in May 2020 following heavy rains, causing major flooding in Midland County
Historical Significance: Built by Frank Isaac Wixom, privately owned by Boyce Hydro Power, license terminated in 2018
Current Status: Dam breached, federal license terminated, Michigan EGLE oversight

Impact: Massive flood wave, widespread destruction, rebuilt by forced labor
Construction Details: Hydroelectric dam spanning the Eder river, constructed between 1908 and 1914, rebuilt after being breached in World War II
Cause of Failure: Dam breached by Allied bombs during World War II, rebuilt during the war
Historical Significance: Dam breached during World War II, rebuilt quickly, third-largest reservoir in Germany
Current Status: Generates hydroelectric power, regulates water levels, major recreational facility

Impact: 5 houses washed away, bridge disabled, roads blocked, 4 bodies discovered
Construction Details: Earth-fill embankment dam on the Ebana River, primary purpose was irrigation
Cause of Failure: Dam failed after 2011 Tōhoku earthquake, reservoir overtopped dam's crest
Historical Significance: Construction halted due to World War II, failed after earthquake
Current Status: Dam inaccessible for inspection, breach at tallest section, improper foundation preparation, severe slope failure in auxiliary dam

Location: Valle di Scalve, Province of Bergamo, Italy
Impact: Widespread flooding, villages flooded, 4,500,000 m3 of water released
Construction Details: Multiple arch buttress dam on the Gleno Creek, built between 1916 and 1923 for hydroelectric power, 3,728 kW installed capacity
Cause of Failure: Middle section of the dam collapsed, causing widespread flooding, at least 356 deaths
Historical Significance: Proposed and constructed by Viganò company, change in design due to lack of funding
Current Status: Dam collapsed, causing significant loss of life and property damage

Location: Dale Dyke Dam, Bradfield Dale near Low Bradfield, Sheffield, England
Impact: Over 600 houses damaged or destroyed, reforms in engineering practice
Construction Details: Constructed by the Sheffield Waterworks Company in the Loxley Valley, 3 million cubic meters of water released
Cause of Failure: Dam failure during first filling, cause of crack in embankment unknown
Historical Significance: Led to reforms in engineering practice, standards for large-scale structures
Current Status: Dam rebuilt in 1875

Impact: Farmland devastated, livestock killed, bridge destroyed, temporary shelter provided
Construction Details: Holds a reservoir on the Sokoto River, supplies water to Gusau city and neighboring communities
Cause of Failure: Dam collapsed after heavy flooding, insufficient to meet local needs in times of drought
Historical Significance: Requests for assistance from Federal Government, mismanagement allegations, heavy rainfall caused collapse
Current Status: Dam collapsed, 700 people temporarily sheltered, no significant repairs or improvements mentioned

Location: Missouri River, near Helena, Montana, United States
Impact: Reservoir formed is Hauser Lake, 25 miles long, surface area of 3,800 acres, storage capacity of 98,000 acre-feet
Construction Details: Original dam built between 1905 and 1907, failed in 1908, second dam opened in 1911, current dam 700 feet long and 80 feet high
Cause of Failure: Original dam failure caused severe flooding and damage downstream
Historical Significance: Named after Samuel T. Hauser, former Territorial Governor of Montana
Current Status: Dam currently generates 17 MW of electricity, considered a "run-of-the-river" dam

Impact: Lake drained in 2010, current dam completed in 2018, impounded water earlier than planned
Construction Details: Concrete gravity dam on Little Rockfish Creek, created Hope Mills Lake, first dam built in 1839, second dam in 1924, current dam completed in early 2018
Cause of Failure: First dam failed in 1923 due to flooding, second dam breached in 2010, current dam developed a leak in 2010
Historical Significance: Dam played a historical role in powering cotton mills, multiple dam failures and repairs
Current Status: Current dam completed in 2018, operational

Impact: Legal changes to strict liability, American Red Cross relief effort
Construction Details: South Fork Dam on the south fork of the Little Conemaugh River, 14.55 million cubic meters of water released
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure after heavy rainfall, 2,209 people killed, $17 million in damages
Historical Significance: Legal changes to strict liability, American Red Cross relief effort
Current Status: Dam no longer exists, legal changes implemented

Impact: Homes destroyed, flood raced downhill through a ravine
Cause of Failure: Dam burst due to heavy rain, flood destroyed homes, killed 7 people, including a toddler and a pregnant woman
Historical Significance: Dam burst led to indictments and civil cases, lack of dam inspections and maintenance
Current Status: Settlement reached in civil cases, owner indicted for manslaughter and reckless endangerment

Impact: Destruction of over 1,600 houses, 2,000 acres of paddy fields, affecting over 8,000 families
Construction Details: Large embankment dam, 14,000 ft long, over 50 ft high
Cause of Failure: Dam breach leading to over 120 deaths
Historical Significance: Built by King Aggabodhi II and developed by King Parakramabahu the Great
Current Status: Reconstructed after the breach

Impact: Dam never rebuilt, memorial to the victims at Toccoa Falls
Construction Details: Earthen embankment dam, originally a rock crib dam in 1899, raised and modified multiple times, 38 feet high, 400 feet long, 20 feet wide
Cause of Failure: Collapse due to heavy rainfall, 39 deaths, $2.8 million in damage
Historical Significance: Historical hydroelectric power plant site, recreational lake
Current Status: Dam not rebuilt, memorial in place

Impact: Miles of fertile farmland covered with river sand, disruption of rural economy, annual floods affecting 21,000 km2 of land
Construction Details: Sluice across the Koshi river with 56 gates, built between 1958 and 1962 by Joseph and Company Limited, India
Cause of Failure: Eastern embankments collapsed in August 2008, causing widespread flooding and displacement
Historical Significance: Known as the "Sorrow of Bihar," affects Terai region, home to Gangetic Dolphin, Siberian birds, popular tourist spot
Current Status: Repaired and operational, continues to be a key transportation route between Saptari and Sunsari districts
Casualties: 53,800 Nepalese affected, 3 million people homeless in Bihar, India

Impact: Hit Kurenivka neighborhood, residential area, tram depot, industrial buildings, cemetery, and traffic
Construction Details: Dam securing loam pulp dump of a brick factory near Babi Yar ravine collapsed after rain, releasing pulp sludge, mud, water, and human remains
Cause of Failure: Mudslide caused by dam collapse, dumped mud, water, and human remains into Kyiv streets
Historical Significance: Soviet authorities suppressed information, claimed 145 deaths, forbade memorial events
Current Status: Recovery operations led by Soviet Army, events censored by Soviet Government

Impact: Torrents of water 2 meters high, washed away a bridge, emergency services provided aid and shelter
Cause of Failure: Dam burst due to torrential rain and snowmelt, flooding the village
Casualties: At least 43 killed, 211 injured, over 1000 evacuated
Response and Investigation: Investigation initiated, criminal probe ordered by President Nazarbayev

Impact: Downstream communities received proper warning, no additional casualties
Construction Details: Gravity dam on the Göksu River, primary purpose is hydroelectric power generation, construction began in 2009
Cause of Failure: Dam's diversion tunnel seal broke during impoundment, 97,000,000 m3 of water flooded downstream area
Historical Significance: Dam failure resulted in worker fatalities, no civilian casualties
Current Status: Dam completed in 2012, operational for hydroelectric power generation

Impact: Lake left empty, refilled after repairs, popular tourist attraction in Wisconsin Dells area
Construction Details: Man-made freshwater lake formed in 1927, part of a dike wall eroded due to floods in 2008
Cause of Failure: County highway portion eroded, causing lake to empty, major repairs completed by March 2009
Historical Significance: Popular tourist attraction, site of Tommy Bartlett's Thrill Show
Current Status: Completely refilled and usable since Memorial Day weekend of 2009

Impact: 9 homes destroyed, 19 damaged, $5.3 million in damages
Construction Details: Earthen embankment dam, largest reservoir of seven dams to fail during the Johnstown Flood of 1977
Cause of Failure: Dam failure during heavy rain, 101 million US gallons of water flooded downstream Tanneryville, 40 fatalities
Historical Significance: Part of the Johnstown Water Company's infrastructure, abandoned Laurel Run reservoir No. 1 in 1910
Current Status: Dam failed, no longer exists

Location: Rocky Mountain National Park, Colorado, United States
Impact: Flash flood, killed campers, damaged Estes Park and downstream areas
Construction Details: Originally a natural lake, dam built in 1903 by farmers from Loveland, elevation of 11,000 feet
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to deterioration of lead caulking on outlet pipe joint, internal erosion of earth-fill dam
Historical Significance: Lack of inspection and repairs, issues reported in previous inspections
Current Status: Dam no longer exists, remote and difficult location led to lapses in maintenance

Location: Reyran River, north of Fréjus, French Riviera
Impact: Collapsed, resulting in a flood, signs of imminent collapse observed in November 1959
Construction Details: Arch dam, doubly curved equal angle arch type, variable radius, 222m width, 66m height, 6.78m base thickness, 1.5m rim thickness
Cause of Failure: Tectonic fault in impermeable rock base, inadequate survey, possible contribution from nearby road-building works
Historical Significance: Led by engineer André Coyne, construction cost 580 million francs, funded by Var département
Current Status: Dam collapsed, no longer exists

Impact: Largest recorded damage, 43.7 million cubic meters of mine tailings released, toxic brown mudflow polluted Doce River and Atlantic Ocean
Construction Details: Fundão tailings dam at the Germano iron ore mine of the Samarco Mariana Mining Complex
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure, flooding downstream villages of Bento Rodrigues and Paracatu de Baixo, pollutants spread along 668 km of watercourses
Historical Significance: Humanitarian crisis, extensive litigation, government sanctions, charges filed against executives
Current Status: Officially under investigation, unclear total impact, ongoing controversy, owner Samarco subject to legal actions

Impact: Polluted hundreds of miles of rivers, one of the worst environmental disasters in the southeastern US
Construction Details: Coal slurry impoundment owned by Massey Energy, bottom broke into an abandoned underground mine below
Cause of Failure: Bottom of impoundment broke, releasing 306 million US gallons of slurry containing arsenic and mercury
Historical Significance: EPA ordered cleanup and restoration, ongoing water quality issues, new regulations implemented
Casualties: Contaminated water supply for over 27,000 residents, killed all aquatic life in affected areas

Impact: Damage to several homes, undermined section of Route 140
Construction Details: Earthen dam constructed by the Bergerons, 465 feet long, 36 feet high, expanded Meadow Pond to 45 acres
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to a three-foot hole opening, releasing 92 million gallons of water
Historical Significance: Fatal flood caused by dam failure
Current Status: Dam collapsed, fatal flood occurred

Impact: Environmental damage, contamination of water bodies
Construction Details: Tailings dam breach at the Mount Polley mine, spill of mine tailings into nearby lake and creek
Cause of Failure: Dam breach caused by tailings spill, environmental contamination
Historical Significance: Significant environmental impact, mining industry scrutiny
Current Status: Ongoing environmental remediation and monitoring

Impact: Significant floodwave, over 800 victims in Neheim-Hüsten, impact on German industry and civil population
Construction Details: Largest dam in Europe, built between 1908-1913, 135 million cubic meters of water storage
Cause of Failure: Dam breached by RAF Lancaster Bombers during Operation Chastise, hole of 77m x 22m blown into the dam
Historical Significance: Largest dam in Europe at the time, built to control floods, regulate water levels, generate hydropower
Current Status: Dam quickly repaired by Organisation Todt, impact on German industry and civil population significant
Casualties: At least 1,579 deaths, including 1,026 foreign forced laborers

Location: Opuha Dam, Opuha River, South Canterbury, New Zealand
Impact: Creation of Lake Opuha, installation of aeration system in 2003
Construction Details: Water storage for farming irrigation, 7.7 MW electricity generation, Important Bird Area supporting black-billed gull colonies
Cause of Failure: Dam failure during construction on February 6, 1997 due to heavy rain, completed in 1998
Historical Significance: Identified as an Important Bird Area, supports endangered bird species
Current Status: Completed in 1998, ongoing maintenance and aeration system in place

Impact: Massive flooding in Pune, estimated 1,000 deaths, popular picnic spot and water sports center
Construction Details: Dam on the Ambi river, part of Mutha River, constructed in late 1950s for irrigation, burst due to absence of reinforced cement concrete strengthening in the conduit
Cause of Failure: Dam wall burst, plain unreinforced concrete blocks used, causing massive flooding in Pune, approximately 1,000 deaths
Historical Significance: Approximately 1,000 deaths, significant impact on Pune
Current Status: Dam burst in 1961, remains a popular tourist spot and water sports center

Impact: Massive flooding, part of a larger flood event affecting nearly a million people in the region
Construction Details: Privately owned embankment dam, part of a commercial rose farm, capacity of 200,000 cubic meters
Cause of Failure: Dam burst amid heavy rains, killing at least 48 people
Historical Significance: Under investigation for legality and safety standards
Current Status: Dam burst, investigation ongoing

Impact: CA$1.5 billion in damages, 488 homes destroyed, 1,230 damaged, 10 additional deaths from mudslides
Construction Details: Geological graben region, two weeks of constant rain, over 8 feet of rainfall, poorly maintained dikes and dams
Cause of Failure: Biggest overland flood in 20th-century Canadian history, flash floods due to engorged soils and rivers
Historical Significance: Poor maintenance of protective infrastructure, symbol of survival in a small white house
Current Status: Preserved as a historical park and museum, new sediments covering contaminated river bottoms

Location: Yenisei River, near Sayanogorsk, Khakassia, Russia
Construction Details: Largest power plant in Russia, 6,400 MW capacity, arch-gravity dam 242 meters high
Historical Significance: Largest power plant in Russia, 9th largest hydroelectric plant in the world

Location: Sempor Dam, District Gombong, Kebumen, Central Java Province, Indonesia
Impact: Widespread damage, irrigation source for paddy fields, flood control, tourist attraction
Construction Details: Embankment dam on the Sempor River, construction started in 1967, 1 MW hydroelectric power station
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to overtopping from flash floods, 160 deaths in Magelang and surrounding towns
Historical Significance: Initial failure in 1967, reconstruction completed in 1978, power station commissioned in 1980
Current Status: Dam completed and operational, serves irrigation and power generation purposes

Impact: Villagers drowned and dragged into the Arabian Sea
Construction Details: Dam constructed in 2003, 485 meters long, built at a cost of 45 million Pakistani Rupees
Cause of Failure: Dam burst due to heavy flooding from heavy rainfall
Historical Significance: Dam provided irrigation for nearby farms
Current Status: Dam burst, emergency search and rescue operations conducted by the Pakistani military

Impact: Dam no longer used for flood control, embankment cofferdam built to prevent water flow, collapsed section retained as a memorial
Construction Details: Concrete gravity barrage dam built from 1974 to 1977, 35.2 meters high, 357 meters long, original capacity of 3,380,000 cubic meters
Cause of Failure: Dam heavily damaged in the 921 earthquake of 1999, collapse of northern end
Historical Significance: Dam heavily damaged in 1999 earthquake, reduced storage capacity, important source of agricultural water
Current Status: Dam no longer used for flood control, remains an important source of agricultural water

Location: Silver Lake Dam, Woodridge, New York, United States
Impact: Dam regulates Sandburg Creek, created 85-acre Silver Lake, attraction for visitors, listed on National Register of Historic Places
Construction Details: Stone structure built in the 1840s, 176 feet long, 5 feet wide, 13 feet high, centrally located spillway, two 15-inch cast-iron waste pipes
Cause of Failure: Leak due to deteriorating condition, repair work initiated in the early 21st century
Historical Significance: Listed on National Register of Historic Places, attraction for visitors to Jewish summer resorts
Current Status: Repair work initiated due to a leak in the dam

Location: Situ Gintung, Cirendeu, South Tangerang, Indonesia
Impact: Flooding, destruction of cars, houses, and infrastructure, standing water up to 2.5 meters deep
Construction Details: Earth dam built by Dutch colonial authorities in 1933, up to 16 meters high, reservoir held 2,000,000 cubic meters of water
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to overtopping, erosion, and breach caused by heavy rain, inadequate spillway gate
Historical Significance: Complaints about leaks, breach in November 2008, lack of maintenance
Current Status: Dam failed, ongoing maintenance and safety measures likely implemented

Location: Boyd County and Holt County, Nebraska
Impact: Heavy flooding downstream, initial construction completed in 1927
Construction Details: Run of the river hydroelectric dam on the Niobrara River, completed in 1927, 3,698 feet long, 26 feet high, powerhouse with two generators, spillway with tainter gates
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to heavy flooding from March 2019 North American blizzard
Historical Significance: Initial construction in 1927, rebuilt in 1940 after partial breach, filled with sediment over the years
Current Status: Dam failed in 2019, no longer operational, heavy flooding downstream

Location: San Francisquito Canyon, Los Angeles County, California, United States
Impact: Considered one of the worst American civil engineering disasters, second-greatest loss of life in California history
Construction Details: Concrete gravity dam built from 1924 to 1926, part of Los Angeles Aqueduct, designed by William Mulholland
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure due to defective soil foundation and design flaws, triggering a flood
Historical Significance: Ended William Mulholland's career, significant loss of life and property damage
Current Status: Dam no longer exists, remains a historical site of a major disaster

Impact: Used for flood control, water storage, and recreation
Construction Details: Masonry arch dam, 108 feet high, 960-acre Sweetwater Reservoir, part of a system for water supply
Historical Significance: Dam raised and retrofitted over the years, survived a flood in 1916
Current Status: Operational, in use for its intended purposes

Location: Lewis River, Skamania County, Washington, United States
Impact: Contributed to the development of the region, provided power supply
Construction Details: Earth-type hydroelectric dam, part of the Swift Reservoir
Historical Significance: Significant structure in Washington state, hydroelectric power generation

Impact: Board of inquiry findings, volcano not erupting at the time
Construction Details: Collapse of tephra dam holding back Mount Ruapehu's crater lake, rapid mudflow in Whangaehu River, bridge pier collapse
Cause of Failure: 151 killed
Historical Significance: Worst rail accident in New Zealand, bridge collapse
Casualties: Locomotive and first six carriages derailed into river, New Zealand's worst rail accident

Impact: Dam cost $100 million to build, $300 million in federal claims, total damage estimates up to $2 billion
Construction Details: Earthen dam built by the Bureau of Reclamation, 310 feet high, 0.6 miles long, intended for hydroelectric power
Cause of Failure: Catastrophic failure during first filling, 11 human deaths, 16,000 livestock deaths
Historical Significance: Dam not rebuilt, interest in controlling spring runoff and providing constant water supply
Current Status: Dam not rebuilt, significant financial and environmental impact

Location: Sank River, Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, India
Construction Details: Constructed in 1916, 24 meters high, 1341 meters long, capacity of 4.8 million cubic meters
Historical Significance: Previous dam failure in 1917
Current Status: Operational, supplying water to the city

Impact: Flooding of downstream villages, houses washed away
Construction Details: Constructed around 2000
Cause of Failure: Dam failure due to heavy rains, flooding downstream villages
Current Status: Investigation ordered, rescue operations conducted

Location: Tokwe Mukorsi Dam, Masvingo Province, Zimbabwe
Impact: Construction suspended in June 2014 due to lack of funding, resumed in May 2016, completed in December 2016, commissioned in May 2017
Construction Details: Concrete-face rock-fill dam on the Tokwe River, 90.3 meters tall, 1,750,000,000 m3 reservoir, 12 MW hydroelectric power station
Cause of Failure: Partial failure on downstream face in February 2014 due to heavy flooding, evacuations upstream, over 20,000 people evacuated
Historical Significance: Dam failure led to evacuations, ongoing water settlement issue
Current Status: Completed and commissioned in May 2017, water settlement ongoing

Impact: Notable flood event in the Valencian Community
Construction Details: Dam on the river Jucar, failed due to overtopping during severe rainfall, operator unable to open spillway gate
Cause of Failure: Over 30 casualties
Historical Significance: Dam failed, current status unknown
Casualties: Flood of 16,000 m3/s, known as La Pantanada

Location: Vajont Dam, Erto e Casso, Italy
Impact: Massive flooding, destruction of villages and towns, dam remained almost intact
Construction Details: Tallest dam in the world at 262m, built between 1957 and 1960 by Società Adriatica di Elettricità
Cause of Failure: Landslide during initial filling caused megatsunami, overtopping dam and flooding Piave Valley
Historical Significance: ENEL and Italian government dismissed warnings, concealed reports, underestimated landslide size
Current Status: Dam remains intact, two-thirds of water retained behind dam

Impact: Town of Ribadelago quickly engulfed by floodwater, debris carried along, insufficient time to escape
Construction Details: Dam constructed over the Tera River, 33 meters high, spanning 380 meters, capacity of 7.8 million cubic meters
Cause of Failure: Failure of a section of the dam's retaining wall, releasing 7.8 million cubic meters of water
Historical Significance: First of two fatal dam failures in Europe in 1959, part of the Montes de León mountain range
Current Status: Dam failed, resulting in fatalities, managed by Hidroeléctrica Moncabril

Location: Viejo River near El Hato de La Virgen, Matagalpa Department, Nicaragua
Impact: Severe damage to the dam, over-topping of spillways, nearly destroyed
Construction Details: Embankment dam supporting the 50 MW Carlos Fonesca Plant, completed in 1972
Cause of Failure: Dam destroyed in 1998 due to heavy rains and flooding from Hurricane Mitch, later rebuilt
Historical Significance: Dam was rebuilt after the destruction
Current Status: Operational after reconstruction

Impact: Severe damage to main dam, replacement of secondary dam with rock-fill dam, rehabilitation costing US$30 million
Construction Details: Clay-fill embankment dam on Wadi Al-Qattara, 33 km east of Benghazi, secondary dam downstream, seven drop structures
Cause of Failure: Flood in 1979 severely damaged main dam, destroyed secondary dam
Historical Significance: Constructed after floods in 1938 and 1954, designed by Coyne et Bellier
Current Status: Main dam rehabilitated, secondary dam replaced between 2000-2004

Impact: 80km2 area engulfed, 251 homes destroyed, hundreds damaged
Construction Details: Embankment dam completed in 1996, impounded water from the Orontes River for irrigation, reservoir filled in winter
Cause of Failure: Dam failed due to cracks in the embankment, 80m wide hole breached, releasing a 3.3m tall wave of water
Historical Significance: Syrian officials ignored warnings, aid sent by international organizations
Current Status: Dam failed, significant casualties and property damage, aid sent by various entities
